Codecov Setup Guide

Source: CODCOV_SETUP.md

What is Codecov?

Codecov is a code coverage tracking service that measures what percentage of your code is tested by your test suite. It:

  • Integrates with your CI/CD pipeline (like GitHub Actions)
  • Analyzes test coverage reports generated by covr (for R packages)
  • Provides visual reports showing which lines of code are covered by tests
  • Tracks coverage over time
  • Shows coverage changes in pull requests

Benefits

  • Identify untested code: See which functions/lines aren’t covered by tests
  • Track progress: Monitor how coverage improves over time
  • Quality assurance: Ensure new code includes tests
  • Visual feedback: Coverage reports in pull requests

Setup Steps

1. Install Required Packages

Make sure you have covr installed (it’s used to generate coverage reports):

install.packages("covr")

2. Add GitHub Actions Workflow

I’ve created a GitHub Actions workflow file (.github/workflows/test-coverage.yml) that will: - Run your tests on multiple R versions - Generate coverage reports using covr - Upload coverage to Codecov

3. Connect to Codecov

  1. Go to codecov.io and sign up with GitHub
  2. Add your repository: metapsy-project/metapsyTools
  3. Codecov will automatically detect coverage reports from GitHub Actions

4. Add Badge to README

The badge has been added to your README. It will show: - Current test coverage percentage - Link to detailed coverage report

The badge format is:

[![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/metapsy-project/metapsyTools/branch/main/graph/badge.svg)](https://codecov.io/gh/metapsy-project/metapsyTools)

5. Generate Coverage Locally

You can test coverage locally before pushing:

# Install covr if not already installed
install.packages("covr")

# Generate coverage report
covr::package_coverage()

# View coverage report in browser
covr::report()

How It Works

  1. GitHub Actions runs: When you push code or create a PR, GitHub Actions runs your tests
  2. Coverage is generated: covr analyzes which code is executed during tests
  3. Report is uploaded: Coverage data is sent to Codecov
  4. Badge updates: The badge in your README automatically updates with the latest coverage percentage
  5. PR comments: Codecov comments on PRs showing coverage changes

Coverage Goals

  • Good: 70-80% coverage
  • Excellent: 80-90% coverage
  • Outstanding: 90%+ coverage

Don’t aim for 100% - some code (like error handlers, edge cases) may be difficult to test, and that’s okay!

Troubleshooting

  • Badge not showing: Make sure the workflow has run at least once and uploaded coverage
  • Coverage seems low: Check which files have low coverage and add tests
  • Workflow fails: Check GitHub Actions logs for errors

Additional Resources

  • covr documentation
  • Codecov documentation
  • GitHub Actions for R

On this page
